1: Psalm 5 is a morning petition directed toward the temple. 'Give ear to my words; consider my groaning' — hagig connotes the wordless sigh or inarticulate groan beneath speech. The psalmist presents not only the formulated petition but the pre-verbal emotional content driving it. In the ANE, approach to a deity was highly formalized; the petitioner was expected to address the deity with honorific titles, recitations of past benefits, and properly structured requests. The Psalter's openness to presenting raw grief and wordless groaning to YHWH — as legitimate prayer — reflects Israel's covenant intimacy with her God rather than the formal distance of ANE cultic protocol.
2: 'My King and my God, for to you I pray' — a political-theological exclusivity claim: YHWH, not any earthly suzerain or any member of the divine pantheon, is the ultimate sovereign to whom petition is exclusively directed. In Mesopotamia, petitioners routinely approached multiple deities; a prayer to Marduk might also invoke Ishtar as intercessor. Israel's exclusive prayer to YHWH was the practical expression of the First Commandment ('You shall have no other gods before me') in the domain of worship. The political vocabulary — 'my King' — asserts that YHWH's rule is not merely religious but governmental.
3: 'In the morning I lay my prayer before you and wait' — morning prayer corresponded to the daily morning sacrifice at the tabernacle/temple (Exod 29:38-41; Num 28:3-8: a yearling lamb with grain offering at dawn every day, without exception). The word erech (lay, arrange) is the technical term for arranging wood or the portions of sacrifice on the altar. The psalmist's verbal petition is itself a form of morning offering. The act of 'waiting' (tsapah, watch like a sentinel on the city wall) carries the image of the watchman scanning the horizon for the expected answer.
4: 'You are not a God who takes pleasure in wickedness; evil cannot dwell with you' — the moral character of YHWH distinguishes Israel's deity from surrounding deities who could be manipulated by ritual regardless of the worshiper's ethical conduct. Mesopotamian deities could be appeased through the correct performance of ritual; the petitioner's moral character was secondary to technical correctness. Israel's theology insisted that YHWH's holiness created an incompatibility with moral evil that no ritual could bridge without genuine moral alignment. This was a revolutionary claim in the religious landscape of the ancient Near East.
5: 'The arrogant cannot stand in your sight; you hate all who practice evil' — the temple threshold as the site where ritual and moral purity was required. Ps 24:3-4 frames the same principle: 'Who may ascend the hill of the LORD? Who may stand in his holy place? He who has clean hands and a pure heart.' The admission of the morally compromised to the temple without repentance would violate the holiness of the divine space. This ethical threshold for worship was the social-religious complement to the ritual purity laws: both guarded the sanctity of the divine presence.
6: 'You destroy those who speak falsehood; the LORD abhors the violent and the deceitful' — false witness was a capital crime in Israelite covenant law (Deut 19:16-21: a false witness receives the punishment they intended to inflict on the accused). In a society where oral testimony was the primary legal instrument — no recording devices, no photographs, no forensic science — the integrity of witnesses was the foundation of the entire justice system. YHWH's judicial hostility to false speech is grounded in the covenant order's complete dependence on truthful testimony.
7: 'Through the abundance of your steadfast love (hesed), I will come into your house. I will bow down toward your holy temple in reverence of you' — hesed is the covenant term for YHWH's committed loyalty to Israel under treaty obligation; it is not mere sentiment but the contractual faithfulness grounding all petitions. The basis for temple access is YHWH's hesed, not the worshiper's merit. Bowing toward the temple (or later, toward Jerusalem) became the prescribed posture for prayer (1 Kgs 8:29-30; Dan 6:10: Daniel prayed toward Jerusalem three times daily), orienting the worshiper toward the place where YHWH's name dwelt.
8: 'Lead me in your righteousness because of those who lie in wait for me; make your way level before me' — the path (derek) metaphor for the righteous life requires divine guidance to navigate. The 'enemies who lie in wait (shoreriai, my watchers)' are likely false accusers using the legal system as a weapon. In ancient Israel, a false accuser could bring testimony before the elders at the city gate (Deut 19:15-21); without the divine judge's guidance, an innocent person could be destroyed by coordinated false testimony. The petition is for YHWH to make the right path clear against the schemes of those who try to divert the psalmist into actions that would justify their accusations.
9: 'There is nothing reliable in their mouth; their inward part is ruin; their throat is an open grave; they flatter with their tongue' — the open grave (qever patu'ah) is ritually unclean under Levitical law (Num 19:16: contact with a grave or bone produces seven days of uncleanness). Speech from the morally corrupt is contaminating like a corpse. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 3:13 within a catena of OT texts about universal human sinfulness, demonstrating that what the Psalm applied to the psalmist's specific enemies the NT reads as the universal condition of fallen humanity — not an aberration but a pattern.
10: 'Hold them guilty, O God; let them fall by their own schemes; drive them away for the multitude of their rebellions, for they have defied you' — the imprecatory petition operates within the framework of covenant justice, not personal revenge. The psalmist does not take revenge but petitions YHWH as the authorized divine judge to apply covenant sanctions. The ultimate basis shifts from personal offense to theological: 'they have defied you' — the wrongdoing is primarily against YHWH's covenant order, not merely against the psalmist. This framing distinguishes covenant justice-seeking from personal vendetta.
11: 'Let all who take refuge in you rejoice; let them shout for joy forever' — the asylum language of covenant protection. The temple was literally a place of physical asylum: 1 Kgs 1:50-51 records Adonijah grabbing the horns of the altar to seek sanctuary; 2 Kgs 11:15 specifies that Joash was not to be killed 'in the house of the LORD.' The Psalter extends this physical asylum into a comprehensive metaphor for covenant protection under YHWH's patronage. 'Those who love your name' — love of the divine name is love of all that YHWH has revealed himself to be; the name encapsulates character and relationship.
12: 'You surround him with favor (ratson) as with a shield' — divine favor as military protection. Ratson (good pleasure, approval, delight) is the royal word for the king's pleasure toward a subject (Esth 1:8; 2:4); YHWH's favor toward the righteous is the supreme form of this royal approval. The image of the favored person being encircled by YHWH's ratson like a battle shield represents the ultimate social reversal in the honor-shame world: honor derived not from human estimation (which the enemies attack) but from divine approval (which cannot be taken away by human accusation).
